# Methodology Appendix: Evidence Handling, Claim Discipline, and Editorial Safety Gates

## Purpose of the methodology

This methodology appendix describes how evidence should be gathered, classified, reviewed, and translated into public-facing prose for the Terefo Heal Reboa book pipeline. Its purpose is not to prove that loop engineering is already a settled field, nor to claim that any named tool, practice, or organizational pattern has achieved broad adoption. Instead, the methodology establishes a disciplined boundary between internal pipeline evidence and claims that may eventually appear in a professional or academic manuscript.

The manuscript’s central thesis is that agent loops should be treated as governable engineering systems rather than as isolated prompts, demonstrations, or informal automation tricks. That thesis is presented as a working professional and conceptual proposition. The methodology therefore exists to prevent the manuscript from converting local observations, exploratory terminology, tool-specific experience, or development workflow artifacts into stronger claims than the evidence can support. It also provides a repeatable framework for distinguishing between established literature, public documentation, local case material, practitioner interpretation, editorial synthesis, open questions, and unsupported assertions.

This appendix is report-only. It is not a publication approval, not a chapter replacement authorization, and not a signal that book files should be updated. Its function is to draft the prose and procedures that later editorial work may refine, test, and connect to a formal bibliography, literature review, evidence ledger, and conceptual framework.

## Evidence sources and boundaries

The pipeline may draw on several broad categories of evidence, but each category has different evidentiary weight. Potential sources include public technical documentation, public repositories, release notes, standards or policy documents, academic literature, professional articles, conference material, public product material, observed tool behavior in controlled local use, internal workflow logs, pipeline reports, editorial reviews, and discovery notes from public web or social platforms.

The boundary between evidence and book claim is central. An internal note may justify further investigation, but it does not automatically support a reader-facing assertion. A local test may show that a specific workflow behaved in a certain way under specific conditions, but it does not prove general reliability. A vendor or project page may describe a tool’s stated purpose, but it does not independently verify performance, adoption, safety, or maturity. A practitioner observation may be valuable, but it must be labeled as observation unless corroborated by stronger sources.

The manuscript should treat evidence as layered rather than flat. Publicly verifiable sources can support descriptive claims about what a document, repository, or public artifact says. Local pipeline evidence can support bounded claims about what was observed during the project’s own work. Editorial synthesis can connect those materials into a professional argument, but synthesis must not disguise uncertainty. Where evidence is preliminary, uneven, or limited to one local environment, the prose should preserve that limitation.

## Source collection process

Source collection should proceed through deliberate stages. First, the pipeline identifies a research need generated by a chapter outline, conceptual gap, editorial review, or unresolved claim. Second, it searches for public and internal materials relevant to that need. Third, it records enough contextual metadata to allow a later reviewer to understand what kind of material was collected, when it was reviewed, and what role it may play. Fourth, it classifies the source before extracting claims from it. Finally, it separates material suitable for public citation from material that may only guide internal reasoning.

The collection process should favor traceability over volume. A smaller set of clearly categorized sources is preferable to a large inventory of poorly described captures. Each collected source should be handled according to its origin and sensitivity. Public academic and professional sources may be candidates for citation after proper bibliographic handling. Public documentation may support descriptive claims about tool features or stated design. Internal run reports and workflow logs may inform methodology or case reflection, but should not be presented as independent external validation. Social discovery material may point toward topics, actors, or terminology worth investigating, but should not be treated as substantive evidence unless independently verified through appropriate sources.

Collection should also include negative and limiting evidence. If searches fail to locate reliable support for a proposed claim, that absence should be recorded as a reason to weaken, defer, or remove the claim. If sources conflict, the conflict should be preserved rather than smoothed away prematurely. The collection process should produce a more cautious manuscript, not merely a more decorated one.

## Inclusion and exclusion criteria

A source may be included in the evidence base when it is relevant to a defined manuscript question, sufficiently attributable for internal review, and classifiable by source type and quality. Inclusion does not mean that the source is suitable for citation or that it supports a strong claim. It means only that the source may contribute to analysis under controlled conditions.

Sources should be prioritized when they are public, stable, attributable, and directly relevant to the manuscript’s concepts. Academic literature, technical standards, well-maintained documentation, public repositories with inspectable history, and carefully bounded local case material may all be useful. Professional commentary may also be useful when presented as commentary and not as proof of consensus.

Sources should be excluded or quarantined when they are unverifiable, misleadingly promotional, unrelated to the research question, inaccessible for later review, legally or ethically inappropriate to use, or dependent on private information that cannot be safely transformed into public prose. Raw personal data, private communications, login-restricted content, and unredacted captures should not be inserted into the manuscript. Materials gathered for operational troubleshooting should not be converted into public claims unless they have been reviewed for privacy, relevance, and evidentiary fit.

Claims should be excluded when their support rests only on inference, excitement, analogy, or internal preference. A concept may be proposed as a working frame even when evidence is incomplete, but the prose must then make its status clear. The manuscript should avoid language suggesting settlement, consensus, market validation, field-wide adoption, or proven effectiveness unless strong and appropriate sources support those exact claims.

## Source quality categories

The pipeline should classify sources into quality categories before using them. A proposed taxonomy may include established scholarly sources, public standards or governance sources, primary technical sources, public repository evidence, professional practitioner sources, local case evidence, internal pipeline artifacts, and discovery-only materials.

Established scholarly sources may support claims about prior research, theory, terminology, safety, human oversight, software engineering, operations, observability, or governance. They require proper bibliographic treatment and should be interpreted carefully rather than mined for isolated phrases.

Public standards or governance sources may support claims about regulatory, safety, audit, or assurance contexts. Their scope should be respected. A standard in one domain does not automatically apply to every agentic workflow.

Primary technical sources include official documentation, release notes, architecture notes, and public project material. These can support claims about what a project or vendor says, exposes, or documents. They do not necessarily validate performance or adoption.

Public repository evidence can support bounded claims about visible implementation, maintenance activity, licensing, issues, or examples, if reviewed carefully. Repository activity should not be inflated into community acceptance or production maturity without stronger evidence.

Professional practitioner sources may help identify emerging language and concerns. They are useful for framing but should not be overused as proof.

Local case evidence and internal pipeline artifacts may support reflective methodology and bounded case descriptions. They are not generalizable without caution.

Discovery-only materials, including social search results, should be used only to guide further inquiry.

## Claim extraction and claim status model

Claim extraction should convert source material into proposed manuscript claims only through an explicit status model. Each candidate claim should be treated as provisional until reviewed. The pipeline should identify what the claim says, what kind of evidence supports it, how strong the support is, what limitations apply, and whether the claim belongs in public prose at all.

A practical status model should include at least the following categories. Established claims are supported by strong, relevant, and preferably multiple sources. Descriptive claims report what a source, tool, document, or local process says or did, without generalizing beyond that evidence. Interpretive claims express the author’s synthesis or professional reading of a pattern. Case-bound claims apply only to the manuscript’s own local workflow or a clearly delimited example. Open questions identify unresolved issues for future research or chapters. Unsupported claims lack sufficient backing and should be removed, deferred, or rewritten as questions.

Claim extraction should be conservative. The pipeline should preserve verbs of uncertainty where needed. It should distinguish may, can, appears, suggests, demonstrates in this case, and proves. A local pipeline run may demonstrate that a procedure was followed; it does not prove that the procedure is universally reliable. A public tool example may show that a capability exists; it does not prove that the capability is safe, mature, or widely used.

No raw internal claim identifiers or source identifiers should appear in reader-facing prose. Such identifiers may be useful for internal traceability, but the public manuscript should present clean citations, transparent categories, and careful explanation rather than operational ledger fragments.

## Editorial review and safety gates

The editorial process should include safety gates before any evidence becomes public-facing text. The first gate is relevance: the material must serve a defined chapter purpose. The second is classification: the evidence type and quality category must be known. The third is claim discipline: the proposed wording must match the strength and scope of the evidence. The fourth is privacy and rights review: the material must be safe to use and free of inappropriate personal or restricted content. The fifth is conceptual consistency: the claim must align with the manuscript’s definitions and not introduce unstable terminology without explanation. The sixth is publication readiness: the chapter must be reviewed as a whole for overclaiming, missing context, and unsupported transitions.

These gates should be treated as editorial controls, not bureaucratic decorations. A chapter should not advance merely because text exists. A polished paragraph can still be methodologically unsafe if it depends on a weak source, hides uncertainty, or merges internal workflow evidence with public validation. Conversely, a cautious paragraph may be valuable even when evidence is incomplete, if it clearly states that the topic remains exploratory.

The current draft status does not authorize publication, reader-facing replacement, or direct update of book files. Future publication approval should require a completed methodology, a literature and context apparatus, a stabilized conceptual framework, a bibliography or source-quality appendix, and a review confirming that internal evidence has been translated appropriately.

## Privacy and raw-capture handling

Raw captures, logs, screenshots, transcripts, private notes, and operational artifacts require stricter handling than polished sources. They may contain personal data, credentials, incidental third-party information, private context, or misleading fragments. The pipeline should store and review such material internally only when there is a legitimate editorial or methodological reason to do so. Public prose should use abstraction, paraphrase, aggregation, or omission when raw detail is not necessary.

Private communications should not be quoted or summarized in a way that exposes individuals without permission and clear relevance. Login-protected platform material should not be treated as freely publishable merely because it was visible to the researcher. Operational logs should be screened for secrets, identifiers, and irrelevant personal information. When a raw capture informs a claim, the public manuscript should usually describe the method or observation at a higher level rather than reproduce the capture.

Privacy review should occur before drafting and again before publication. The first review prevents unsafe material from shaping the chapter too directly. The second catches residual traces that may have entered prose during revision.

## Social and LinkedIn discovery-only policy

Social platforms, including LinkedIn, may be useful for discovery. They can reveal terminology, practitioner concerns, examples of public discussion, and potential sources to investigate. However, social discovery should not be treated as substantive evidence for the manuscript unless the material is public, ethically usable, relevant, and corroborated or clearly framed.

The default policy should be discovery-only. Social findings may generate search terms, identify public projects, suggest interview questions, or reveal that a topic is being discussed by practitioners. They should not be used to claim broad adoption, professional consensus, organizational practice, or technical validity. If a social post points to a public paper, repository, standard, or documentation page, the later source may be reviewed on its own merits. The social post itself should normally remain outside the evidentiary core.

This policy protects both the subjects of social content and the integrity of the manuscript. It also prevents the book from mistaking visibility for significance.

## Automation limits

Automation may assist with searching, summarizing, deduplication, classification, drafting, consistency checking, and review prompts. It should not be treated as an authority. Automated summaries can omit nuance, misread context, merge sources, or amplify weak evidence. Automated drafting can make unsupported claims sound coherent. Automated review can miss privacy issues or conceptual drift.

For that reason, automation should remain inside a governed loop. Human editorial judgment, or an equivalent accountable review process, must decide whether a claim is appropriate for public prose. Automation can propose language, but it cannot approve publication. It can flag uncertainty, but it cannot eliminate the need for source review. It can generate a methodology draft, but it cannot transform the draft into a validated research design without further work.

## Reproducibility limits

The pipeline should aim for procedural reproducibility, but it must acknowledge limits. Web pages change, repositories evolve, social feeds are personalized, search results vary, and local tool behavior depends on environment, configuration, timing, and available services. Internal runs may be reproducible only within a specific technical context. Some evidence may be impossible to recapture later in identical form.

The appropriate standard is therefore transparent traceability rather than perfect reproducibility. The pipeline should record methods, dates, categories, and review decisions where possible. It should avoid claims that depend on unstable captures unless the instability is disclosed or the claim is independently supported. Reproducibility limits should lead to caution in wording, especially for claims about trends, adoption, performance, or general practice.

## Bias and limitations

This methodology reflects the manuscript’s current position as a hybrid professional and academic inquiry. It is shaped by local case material, the author’s interests, available tools, selected examples, and the emerging vocabulary of agent-loop practice. Those conditions introduce bias. The manuscript may overrepresent systems visible to the pipeline, tools used locally, public English-language material, and concerns prominent among technical practitioners. It may underrepresent non-public enterprise practice, non-English communities, unsuccessful deployments, labor impacts, accessibility concerns, and perspectives from governance, law, social science, or affected users.

The methodology should not erase these limits. Instead, it should make them visible. A cautious book can still be useful if it names its standpoint, differentiates evidence types, and avoids turning a local vantage point into a universal map.

## Relationship between internal pipeline evidence and public book claims

Internal pipeline evidence can improve the manuscript by showing how the project reasoned, reviewed, and constrained itself. It can support reflective discussion of method, editorial workflow, and case-bound observations. It cannot, by itself, establish field-wide facts. Public book claims should emerge only after internal evidence has been translated through source classification, claim status review, privacy review, and editorial judgment.

The reader does not need access to raw run artifacts to understand the argument. The book should present clear prose, appropriate citations, transparent limitations, and carefully bounded examples. Internal artifacts should remain behind the scenes unless they are intentionally converted into sanitized methodological description.

## What the method cannot prove

This method cannot prove that loop engineering is a settled discipline. It cannot prove that particular tools are safe, mature, widely adopted, or superior. It cannot prove that local automation practices generalize to other organizations. It cannot prove that agent loops are governable in every context. It cannot replace empirical evaluation, security review, legal analysis, user research, or domain-specific validation.

What it can do is support disciplined inquiry. It can help the manuscript ask better questions, avoid overclaiming, preserve uncertainty, and separate conceptual proposal from evidence-backed assertion.

## How future chapters should use evidence

Future chapters should begin by identifying the kind of claim they intend to make. Conceptual chapters should define terms carefully and distinguish proposed vocabulary from established terminology. Literature chapters should connect the manuscript to prior work without pretending that adjacent fields have already endorsed the book’s framing. Case chapters should state the boundaries of the case and avoid generalization beyond observed conditions. Tool chapters should separate public documentation, local experience, and interpretation. Governance chapters should draw from appropriate safety, audit, operations, and oversight sources rather than relying only on technical enthusiasm.

Every chapter should preserve the core discipline of this methodology: evidence first, claim status second, prose third, publication only after review. Until those gates are complete, the manuscript should remain developmental. This report-only methodology draft may guide that work, but it does not approve publication, authorize chapter replacement, or permit insertion of unsupported claims into the public book.
